title: Brad Pitt, Tom Cruise AI Video Backlash Prompts ByteDance To Add Guardrails

Brad Pitt, Tom Cruise AI Video Backlash Prompts ByteDance To Add Guardrails

The homepage of the Chinese AI video generation model Seedance 2.0 appears on a mobile phone, with the Disney logo visible in the background in this photo illustration in Brussels, Belgium, on Feb. 16, 2026. (Photo by Jonathan Raa/NurPhoto via Getty Images)
NurPhoto via Getty Images
Hollywood has been fighting back since ByteDance introduced its Seedance 2.0 model last week, leading to hyperrealistic AI-generated videos that went viral featuring likenesses of Tom Cruise, Brad Pitt and characters from Dragon Ball Z and Pokémon.
As AI tools like Seedance 2.0 create near-perfect video recreations, studios face a new threat. Deepfakes of their stars and characters could appear in unauthorized content without consent, from both professional and amateur creators. Disney claims ByteDance is “hijacking” its protected characters by reproducing, distributing and creating derivative works. Paramount made similar claims. The Motion Picture Association called this copyright infringement at a “massive scale,” and said it undermines “millions of American jobs.” SAG-AFTRA added that actors’ voices and likenesses are used without permission. They call it an “unacceptable” threat to their livelihoods.
This situation resembles past copyright clashes but with a key difference. Earlier AI conflicts often involved static images. Seedance 2.0 creates dynamic video content mimicking real human movement as well as actor’s voices. A viral example showed Tom Cruise and Brad Pitt in an action scene that looked and sounded real. The technology’s sophistication makes it harder for studios to spot AI-generated content.
This increases legal risks for AI makers as well as the companies using their tools.
The Legal Tightrope for AI Developers
ByteDance’s response shows how delicate the position is for AI developers. A company spokesperson said they’re “taking steps to strengthen current safeguards” against “unauthorized use of intellectual property.” This admission reveals a core tension. AI tools need vast data to work. But that data often includes protected content. The company must choose between limiting training data, which could lower quality, or building systems to block unauthorized uses.
MORE FOR YOU
This is by far the first AI copyright clash. In 2023, Getty Images sued Stability AI over image generation. Getty argued the company trained on licensed photos without permission. A judge dismissed the case last November, ruling AI training could be fair use. Video content, especially with recognizable people, is a different legal area. Courts have historically protected celebrity likenesses and character rights more than generic images.
The stakes are high for ByteDance. TikTok’s parent company has invested heavily in AI as a growth driver. But if courts side with studios, it could force major tool redesigns or halt certain uses. This would ripple through the industry. Companies like Meta and Google, developing their own video AI tools, will need to rethink their approaches.
Beyond Hollywood: The Broader Business Implications
The Seedance 2.0 case matters far beyond entertainment. Every company using AI for marketing, ads or product demos must face the reality of their images and brands being used in ways they might not anticipate or accept. Imagine a startup using AI to create videos featuring a famous executive for promotion. Or a social media brand making viral content with product or brand images. Without clear safeguards, these could become legal problems.
The case also highlights a gap in current AI governance. Platforms usually rely on user policies and filters to block bad content. But when AI tools themselves generate content using protected data, those systems often fail. As the MPA noted, the current approach “disregards well-established laws.” This suggests companies need proactive measures, not just reactive filters.
Adobe’s Firefly AI offers a different path. The company built its image generator using licensed data and clear copyright rules. It avoids using real people’s likenesses without permission. While Firefly’s capabilities are more limited than Seedance 2.0, its approach shows a workable solution. Companies that prioritize these safeguards early may avoid ByteDance’s legal issues.
For businesses, AI copyright compliance must be a core requirement. This means auditing training data, building strong filters and setting clear rules for using recognizable elements. Ignoring this could mean lawsuits, damaged brand reputations and lost revenue from content takedowns.
The Seedance 2.0 conflict reflects a shift in how society views AI content. As technology improves, the line between real and synthetic content blurs. This makes consent and attribution even more important. SAG-AFTRA put it simply: “Responsible AI development demands responsibility, and that is nonexistent here.”
The laws might also need an update. Current copyright law was made for static images and text, not dynamic video mimicking living people. Legal experts and tech leaders must work together to create fair rules. One possible solution is mandatory licensing for AI tools using identifiable likenesses. This would work like music streaming royalties.
Companies should also prepare for more transparency demands. As the MPA argued, regulators may soon require clear disclosures about AI content. For ByteDance, the immediate challenge is improving safeguards without losing the tool’s appeal. Its new approach must balance innovation and legal compliance. TikTok has faced similar content moderation issues, but video generation presents a more complex copyright problem.
